title: Best GPS Watch for Running with Music in 2024: Garmin, Apple Watch and More
excerpt: Find the best GPS watch for running with music in 2024. Compare Garmin, Apple Watch, and Coros for Spotify support, battery life, and phone-free running.
body:
The Best GPS Watch for Running with Music: Your Complete Guide to Phone-Free Runs

You're four miles into a solo long run and your playlist is the only thing keeping your legs moving. But your phone is bouncing awkwardly in your shorts, your arm band is slipping, and you're burning mobile data just to stream a single podcast. There is a better way. A GPS watch for running with music solves all three problems at once, putting your tunes, your pace data, and your GPS route on your wrist with nothing else required. This guide breaks down exactly what to look for, which models are worth your money, and how to get the most out of offline listening on the run.

---

Why a GPS Watch for Running with Music Changes Everything

Running with your phone is a compromise. You carry it for safety, for music, for tracking, and it does all three jobs adequately but none of them perfectly. A dedicated GPS watch for running with music handles tracking and audio simultaneously, with better GPS accuracy than most phone apps and storage that lives entirely on your wrist.

The real shift is going phone-free. When you leave your phone at home, your run becomes leaner, lighter, and genuinely more focused. You stop reaching for notifications. You stop adjusting your grip on a device. You just run, and the watch handles everything else.

The practical case is also strong for races. Most road marathons ban headphones or earbuds connected to a phone, but allow standalone music watches paired directly to wireless headphones. Knowing your GPS watch can also serve as your music source means one less piece of gear to manage on race morning.

---

What to Look for in a Running Watch with Music

Onboard Storage Capacity

Storage is the first number to check. Some watches offer as little as 4GB, which holds roughly 1,000 songs but leaves little room for podcasts or audiobooks. Premium models hit 32GB of onboard storage, which is genuinely enough to carry your entire workout playlist library, several podcast episodes, and an offline map of your local trail network all at once.

The minimum you want for a realistic training cycle is 8GB. That gives you room for 2,000 songs plus a few long podcast episodes without having to curate obsessively every time you sync.

GPS Watch Offline Music: Spotify, YouTube Music, and More

Not all music watches support the same streaming services. Garmin music watches typically support Spotify, Amazon Music, Deezer, and iHeartRadio. Apple Watch supports Apple Music and Spotify with offline download. Coros, despite being a strong GPS performer, does not currently offer integrated streaming service support, which means you can load MP3 files manually but cannot sync a Spotify playlist directly from the app.

Spotify compatibility is the feature most runners ask about first, and for good reason. Spotify Premium lets you download playlists directly to a compatible watch, so you can run without streaming data even in areas with poor cell coverage. The sync process typically requires your watch to be connected to Wi-Fi, not just Bluetooth, so plan your playlist prep the night before a big run.

Battery Life During GPS and Music Use

Battery life is where the tradeoffs become very real. A watch that claims 11 days of battery life in smartwatch mode may only deliver 4 to 5 hours when GPS and music playback are running simultaneously. That is enough for most runners, covering anything from a 5K to a full marathon, but it narrows the window for ultra runners or anyone doing back-to-back training days.

Check the GPS-plus-music figure specifically, not the headline battery claim. The Garmin Forerunner 165 Music, for example, delivers 17 hours of GPS battery life in standard GPS mode, but that figure drops when you add continuous music playback. Always plan for less than the advertised maximum if you run in cold conditions, since battery performance degrades below 40 degrees Fahrenheit.

Display Type: AMOLED vs. MIP

AMOLED screens look stunning indoors and are easy to read at a glance mid-run. They consume more power than MIP screens, which is a direct tradeoff against battery life. If you prioritize a full week of training on a single charge, a MIP display may serve you better even if it looks less impressive at the point of sale.

For city runners doing structured workouts where glancing at pace and heart rate matters frequently, the brightness and clarity of an AMOLED screen is worth the battery cost. For trail runners or ultra runners who need 20-plus hours of continuous GPS use, MIP is still the smarter choice.

---

Best GPS Watches for Running with Music in 2024

Garmin Forerunner 165 Music: Best Entry-Level Music Watch

The Garmin Forerunner 165 Music sits at $299.99 and is the most accessible true GPS watch for running with music on the market right now. It has an AMOLED display, onboard storage for Spotify and other streaming services, and 11 days of battery life in smartwatch mode. The GPS battery runs to 17 hours in standard tracking mode, which comfortably covers a marathon with time to spare.

What surprises most first-time users is the quality of the daily training suggestions. The watch analyzes your recent workout history and recommends specific sessions, which is a feature previously reserved for watches costing twice as much. For a runner moving from a basic fitness tracker into structured training, the 165 Music is the logical first step up.

The tradeoff is real. The 165 does not support topographic maps and its recovery metrics are less detailed than those on Garmin's Forerunner 265 or 965. If you run trails heavily and need route navigation, this is not the watch for you. But for road runners who want their Spotify playlists and solid pace data, it delivers exactly what it promises.

Garmin Forerunner 265: Best Mid-Range Balance

The Garmin Forerunner 265 sits a tier above the 165 in both price and capability. It adds training readiness scores, a more comprehensive recovery advisor, and keeps the AMOLED display. Music storage and Spotify support carry over from the 165 Music, but the 265 handles the processing demands more smoothly, with more stable Bluetooth connectivity to wireless headphones during runs.

The 265 is the watch that serious recreational runners tend to buy and keep for several years. It handles everything from daily 5K training runs to half marathon race day, and the training load analysis is granular enough to catch overtraining patterns before they become injuries.

Battery life during GPS and music use is the only point where experienced runners sometimes wish for more. For 50K trail runners or those training for ultramarathons, the 265 is outpaced by its big sibling, the Forerunner 965.

Garmin Forerunner 965: Best Premium Garmin Music Watch

The Garmin Forerunner 965 is where the Garmin music watch lineup matures fully. It pairs 32GB of onboard storage with full mapping capabilities, a titanium bezel, and one of the most complete training metric suites in any GPS running watch. The battery runs up to 31 hours in GPS mode, which covers ultramarathons and multi-day adventures.

Music playback through Spotify, Amazon Music, and Deezer works seamlessly. You can store a dedicated race-day playlist and a separate long-run playlist without deleting anything. At around $599.99, the 965 is a premium investment, but runners who use it daily for years find the cost per run drops quickly.

One honest caveat: the 965 is a large watch by any standard. The case diameter and thickness may feel overwhelming on smaller wrists, and if aesthetics matter to your everyday wear, you should try it on before buying.

Apple Watch for Running: Ultra 3 and Series 11

Apple Watch has become a legitimate option for running, and the Apple Watch Ultra 3 is the most serious version of it. At $800, it is priced against dedicated GPS running watches and holds its own in that comparison for runners who are already deep in the Apple ecosystem.

Apple Music integration is seamless. You can download playlists, albums, and podcasts directly to the watch and stream them via Bluetooth to wireless headphones with no phone required. Spotify offline downloads also work with Spotify Premium.

The running-specific metrics have improved substantially in recent generations. VO2 max estimation, cadence tracking, and vertical oscillation data are all present on the Series 11 and Ultra 3. The Ultra 3 specifically adds a dual-frequency GPS chip, which improves accuracy in urban canyons and dense tree cover, two environments where even Garmin watches can struggle.

The honest tradeoff with Apple Watch for running is battery life. The Series 11 will not get you through a full marathon on GPS-plus-music mode without careful settings management. The Ultra 3 solves this with 36 hours of battery life in standard mode and a low-power mode that stretches further. But if you are training for ultras or doing consecutive long training days, dedicated GPS watches from Garmin and Coros still outrun Apple on endurance.

Garmin Forerunner 570: A New Premium Option

The Garmin Forerunner 570, priced at $549.99, represents the newest direction in Garmin's mid-to-high range lineup. It combines the AMOLED display with advanced training and recovery features that were previously limited to the Fenix series. Music storage and streaming support are included, and the build quality reflects the higher price point.

Early user feedback points to the 570 as a genuinely competitive option for runners who want premium metrics without the bulk of the Fenix 8. It is worth considering if you are choosing between the 965 and a trail-focused adventure watch and land somewhere in the middle.

Coros Pace 4: Best GPS Watch for Runners Who Load Their Own Music

The Coros Pace 4 deserves a prominent mention even though it does not support Spotify or other streaming services natively. At under $250, it delivers 31 hours of battery life in maximum GPS mode, a bright AMOLED display, and genuinely accurate GPS tracking that rivals watches costing twice as much.

For runners who use MP3 files or are willing to use a third-party music loading tool, the Pace 4 becomes an extremely capable GPS watch offline music option. You load your files via the Coros app, pair your wireless headphones, and run. The battery advantage over Garmin's AMOLED options is significant for anyone training for distances above the marathon.

If Spotify integration is non-negotiable, look elsewhere. But if your music library lives in files rather than streaming services, the Coros Pace 4 is one of the most cost-efficient running watches you can buy in 2024.

---

Phone-Free Running: Pairing Your Watch with Wireless Headphones

What Wireless Headphones Work Best

A GPS watch for running with music is only as good as the headphones you pair it with. True wireless earbuds that support Bluetooth 5.0 or later connect more reliably and maintain connection through arm swing, head movement, and sweat. Look for earbuds with an IPX4 rating at minimum, which means they can handle running sweat and light rain without failure.

Bone conduction headphones are worth considering for safety-conscious runners who run near traffic. They sit outside the ear canal entirely, leaving your ambient hearing intact. Audio quality is lower than traditional earbuds but the situational awareness benefit is real, especially on roads without separated running paths.

Most Garmin music watches allow you to control volume and skip tracks directly from the watch face without reaching for your phone. That means you can change your playlist with a few button presses mid-stride, which eliminates one of the main reasons runners still reach for their phones during a run.

How to Sync Spotify to a Garmin Watch

The process takes about ten minutes but needs to be done while your watch is on its charger and connected to Wi-Fi. Open the Garmin Connect app, navigate to the music section, and connect your Spotify Premium account. Select the playlists you want to download. The watch will sync during its next charging session while connected to Wi-Fi.

Important detail: Spotify on Garmin requires a Premium subscription. Free Spotify accounts do not support offline downloads to any device, including watches. Budget that $10.99 monthly cost into your overall setup if you are not already a Premium subscriber.

Once synced, those playlists live entirely on the watch. You do not need cell service, Wi-Fi, or your phone to play them. That is the whole point.

---

Key Considerations Before You Buy

Before committing to a GPS watch for running with music, run through this checklist honestly:

  • What streaming service do you use? If it is Apple Music, Apple Watch is the cleanest integration. If it is Spotify, most Garmin music watches handle it well.
  • How long are your longest runs? If you run ultras or long trail races exceeding 6 hours, prioritize GPS-plus-music battery life over any other feature.
  • Do you need maps? Entry-level music watches like the Forerunner 165 Music skip full mapping. If you run trails without knowing the route by heart, step up to the 965 or Fenix series.
  • What is your budget? Realistic entry point for a capable GPS watch for running with music is $299.99 (Forerunner 165 Music). The ceiling is $800 (Apple Watch Ultra 3 or Garmin Fenix 8 Pro Solar).
  • How important is everyday wearability? Some running watches look like running watches and nothing else. If you wear your watch to work and social events, the Apple Watch Series 11 and newer Garmin AMOLED models blend into daily life far better than the Fenix series.
---

Music Storage Watch vs. Streaming: Which Approach Is Right for You

A music storage watch loads files or syncs playlists in advance. A streaming watch can sometimes pull music live from a connected phone. Most GPS running watches use the first approach, storing tracks locally for offline playback.

The offline approach has a clear advantage in races and remote areas where cell coverage is unreliable. If you race trail 50Ks in mountain terrain, your phone signal disappears long before your legs do. Local storage means your playlist keeps playing regardless of coverage.

The one scenario where phone streaming still wins is variety. If you want to browse new releases mid-run or switch podcasts spontaneously, you still need your phone nearby or a strong cell signal. Pre-downloaded playlists require planning, which is a minor inconvenience that most runners adapt to within a week.

---

Getting the Most Out of Your Running Watch with Spotify

Runners who get the most value from a running watch with Spotify build their playlists with intention before each training block. Create a hard effort playlist for interval sessions with high-BPM tracks that match your target cadence. Build a separate long run playlist with podcasts and lower-tempo music that makes 90 minutes feel manageable.

Sync both playlists at the start of your training week rather than the night before each run. That way you are never stuck waiting for a sync to complete while your shoes are already on.

One detail that catches new users: Spotify occasionally requires re-authentication on Garmin watches after software updates. If your music stops working after a watch update, re-linking your Spotify account in the Garmin Connect app usually resolves it within minutes.

---

Best Running Watches 2024: Summary by Runner Type

For the beginner runner: Garmin Forerunner 165 Music at $299.99. AMOLED screen, Spotify support, 17 hours GPS, and daily training suggestions that grow with you.

For the dedicated road runner: Garmin Forerunner 265 or 965, depending on how seriously you track training load. Both support music, both earn their price over a multi-year training relationship.

For the Apple ecosystem runner: Apple Watch Series 11 for road runners with moderate mileage. Apple Watch Ultra 3 if you run marathons regularly and want the full feature set without compromise.

For the trail and ultra runner: Garmin Forerunner 965 or Fenix 8 series if you want streaming-capable music. Coros Pace 4 if you are willing to load files manually and want maximum battery life at minimum cost.

For the budget-conscious runner who still wants phone-free listening: Coros Pace 4 with manually loaded MP3 files. Not glamorous, but it works for 31 hours straight.

---

Conclusion

A GPS watch for running with music is no longer a luxury for serious athletes. It is a practical tool for any runner who wants clean wrist data, a reliable soundtrack, and the freedom to leave their phone behind. The right choice depends on your streaming service, your race distances, and your budget, but the market has expanded enough that there is a strong option at every price point.

Start with the Forerunner 165 Music if you are new to the category. Step up to the 265 or 965 if your training has a longer horizon. Consider the Apple Watch Ultra 3 if you live in the Apple ecosystem and want a watch that earns its premium price across both running and everyday life. And give the Coros Pace 4 genuine consideration if battery life and GPS accuracy matter more to you than streaming integration.

Whatever you choose, the first phone-free long run with music playing directly from your wrist is one of those small quality-of-life upgrades that makes you wonder why you waited so long.

keyTakeaway: Runners who want to ditch their phones on runs need a GPS watch that can store and stream music independently while accurately tracking their workouts and performance metrics.
